items.title: 💳 Chasing a credit card fee waiver is not always the best strategy. Some examples 👇 1️⃣ Axis Atlas Credit Card In Jan’24, I was offered the Axis Atlas Credit Card as Lifetime Free based on my Burgundy Salary Account. I initially applied for that, but later opted for the paid Atlas instead because it offered 5,000 Edge Miles against the joining fee payment. ✨ 5,000 Edge Miles = 10,000 Accor points, worth ₹20,000+ to me. Getting ₹20,000+ worth of Accor points by paying a joining fee of ₹5,900 was an excellent deal. Even better, during the next two renewals, I received 10,000 Accor points each time against renewal fee payments: ✅ Once for crossing ₹7.5 lakh annual spends. ✅ Once after crossing ₹15 lakh spends in Jan’26. These renewal Accor points against the fee payments would not have been available on the Lifetime Free variant. 2️⃣ HDFC Infinia (when it had 1:1 Accor transfers) When Infinia offered 1:1 Accor point transfers, I didn’t try to meet the ₹10 lakh fee waiver criteria, even though I could have easily done so by preponing a few expenses. Because paying the ₹14,750 renewal fee and receiving 12,500 Accor points (worth Rs.25,000+) was a much better deal for me than getting a fee waiver but missing out on those valuable Accor points. 👉 The key takeaway: Don’t evaluate a credit card only based on whether you can avoid the annual fee. Always compare the renewal benefits, reward value, and your actual usage. In few exceptional cases, paying the fee can deliver significantly higher value than getting it waived. 📘 Visit SpendWisely Blog: Detailed credit card reviews & guides 👉 https://spendwisely.in #creditcard #spendwisely #creditcard

items.title: ⚠️ To all the people who are considering HSBC Demat account route to maintain TRV for Premier Account….. HSBC Demat Account, in its current form, cannot be a substitute for your regular demat account (like Zerodha), because HSBC currently does not offer an associated trading account. 👉 This means that once you transfer your Stocks or ETFs to your HSBC Demat Account, you cannot buy or sell them online like you do with a regular broker. ⏳ Selling a stock or ETF, which usually takes just a few seconds through Zerodha or any other trading platform, can take 5–7 days with HSBC Demat due to the offline process. There are only two ways to transact: 📌 Option 1: Contact your HSBC Relationship Manager (RM) and place an offline buy/sell request. 📌 Option 2: Transfer your Stocks/ETFs back from HSBC Demat to your regular demat account (like Zerodha). For this, you’ll need to fill the physical Delivery Instruction Slip (DIS), courier or hand it over to your RM, who will then initiate the transfer. ⏳ Both processes generally take around 5–7 days to complete. 💡 Earlier, HSBC had a tie-up with ICICI Securities, which allowed customers to buy and sell Stocks/ETFs instantly through an online trading account. That partnership ended last year. HSBC representatives have informed that they are working on bringing back an online trading facility, but there is no confirmed timeline yet. 📈 Most people (including myself) who have chosen the Demat route to maintain the Total Relationship Value (TRV) for the HSBC Premier Account have transferred only that portion of their Stocks/ETF portfolio which they do not intend to sell in the near future and are comfortable keeping locked in an account where selling may take 5–7 days. So, please understand these operational limitations before transferring your investments.
